A weekly audio discussion with Dr. Ivan Misner, the Founder and Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, the world's largest business networking organization.

Episode 711: Choose the Lens You See the World Through

June 16, 2021 by Ivan Misner 32 Comments

Podcast: Download

Subscribe: Apple Podcasts | RSS | Subscription Options

Print This Episode

Synopsis

The lens that we choose to see the world through can greatly influence the things that we see and experience. Words matter: What you put in your head shows up in your world. Difficult situations are real, but even in tough times you can reframe your experience to keep it from overwhelming you.

Examples of Reframing

  • Are you busy or do you have a full life?
  • Do you have cancer or were you diagnosed with cancer?
  • Was 2020 a shutdown or a Great Pause?
  • Are you devastated by grief or putting one foot in front of the other?

Focus on a lens that will help you see through the fog of chaos that surrounds you and look for possibilities. Look for ways to reframe your situation so you can deal most effectively with the hand that you’ve been dealt. And always remember: words matter.

Complete Transcript of Episode 711

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you? And where are you?

Ivan:
Well, I’ve recently been to BNI Greater China, where I did a live event and BNI UK so, opposite ends of the world. And both are absolutely fantastic. We have great Members and Directors in both of those areas.

Priscilla:
Oh, man, that’s wonderful. And tell me what you’re planning to talk to us about today.

Ivan:
Yeah, so I’m talking about choosing the lens that you see the world through. You know, two people can sit in a room at the same table, have the same meal at the same time, and be worlds apart. You may say “How?” but it’s because they look at the world through a different lens.

I love astronomy, I took an astronomy course in college. And I’ve learned that by choosing different lenses or filters for my telescope, I can literally observe different things in the night sky. By just changing the lens, the things I view can appear or disappear before my very eyes. Objects can be overwhelmingly bright and painful to view or they can be a beautiful sight to behold. And I believe our lives are similar. The lens that we choose to see the world through can greatly influence the things that we see and experience.

Years ago, I realized that I needed to evict people from my life because the lens that they viewed the world through was negative, fearful, hurtful, and sometimes caustic. It was not the lens I chose to view the world through. The first thing I realized when I came to this conclusion was that words matter. What you say about your world experience helps determine the world that you live in, and the people you attract into your world or into your network, into your BNI group. What you put in your head shows up in your world. Whether you’re talking about an abundance mentality versus a scarcity mentality, the law of attraction, or the glass being half full, words matter.

The most common example of this from my life involves “being busy”. I talk to so many people who say, “Oh, you must be so busy, Ivan.” I no longer say yes to that question, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Uh-huh.

Ivan:
Today, I say “I have a full life.” You can be busy or you can have a full life. Almost everyone says that their life is busy. But busy shouldn’t be a goal worth pursuing. If it were everyone’s goal, then most individuals would be happy and that’s not what I see with most people. Having a “full life” is a goal worth pursuing and having a “full life” brings me joy. Words truly matter. 

I know that I’ve talked about this in previous podcasts, I now microdose the news. I don’t understand why negative news sells, but it does. And frankly, it’s no longer a “news” program, it’s most often an “opinion” program. Why do we watch something that continuously makes our blood boil? I quit.

I microdose the news through apps on my phone so I can stay up on what’s going on without hearing or viewing all the vitriolic opinions that are being pitched as “the news.”

Back in 2012, I’ve talked about this is my podcast before, I was diagnosed with cancer. But I haven’t talked about this. I never really told people “I have cancer.” I always said I was diagnosed with cancer. It was my diagnosis. It was not who I was. It took a while, but now rather than say I’m in remission, I prefer to say I’m healthy. I’m healthy.

Priscilla:
Mmm-hmm.

Ivan:
Words matter. 2020 brought this out more clearly than ever for me. While everyone was talking about being in “lockdown” or “quarantine”, I spoke about experiencing the “Great Pause” in life. And I’ve talked about that on the podcast, the pause button was pushed on all of our lives for over a year. I found that how people responded to the “Great Pause” made a tangible difference relating to their experience and their life. Those people who learned how to embrace that mentality, when possible, were much happier than those who were mired in the horror of the situation that we were all part of.

My wife passed away, my wife of 31 years passed away in 2020. It would have been easy to say that I was horrible when people asked how I was doing. But instead, I tried to reframe that grief I was experiencing into something that would allow me to have a different worldview. I’d answer friends and associates by telling them that it’s “one day at a time for me,” or “I’m putting one foot in front of the other” each day. Feeling horrible was true. But I also believed that living in that mental space would not help me get through those horrible times. So, one day at a time, one foot in front of the other, was the lens I chose to focus on. So it allowed me to do my best to move forward. And I think this is important. Even when you feel horrible, words matter.                     

We live in fearful times. And I’ve learned that people get frozen by fear, or they get focused by fear. I propose that you, all of you listening to this podcast, get focused by fear. Focus on a lens that will help you see through the fog of chaos that surrounds you and look for possibilities. Look for the positive. Look for how to reframe your situation so you can deal most effectively with the hand that you’ve been dealt. Choose the lens that you see the world through. And always remember that words matter. That’s my message for today, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
That’s a great message, Ivan. We’ve gotten so wise, in the last few years.

Ivan:
Well, it’s all the gray hair.

Priscilla:
Yeah, I choose to be a positive thinker as much as possible. And I know that you can be really influenced by the people around you. And it’s kind of tough. If there are people that see things and talk about things in a negative way a lot, it’s difficult.

Ivan:
Yeah. And, you know, I think it’s important, and I said this in a recent podcast, that it’s not just about, it’s not that you can’t recognize problems, it’s important to recognize problems. But you’ve got to also focus on the solutions. If all you do is focus on the problems, you’re going to be an expert at problems. But you also have to focus on solutions. And so you know, Who’s In Your Room?, the book I wrote, is definitely a book that focuses on surrounding yourself with the right people, surrounding yourself with engines, people who drive you forward, who make you a better person, not anchors, people who are trying to bring you down. Wow, is that not true in a BNI chapter? You want Members who are engines, who help you be your best self, who make you a better person, not anchors, who are constantly trying to bring you down. And whenever I saw that, when I was a Member of BNI when I started it, when I started to grow BNI as an organization, and today more than ever as the Founder and Chief Visionary Officer, I want to surround myself with engines, not anchors. And I want to see the world through a lens of people who are helping me be my best self, not helping to bring me down because they’ve got their own problems and issues and they won’t look for positive ways to address them.

Priscilla:
Right.

Ivan:
Well, that’s everything I have for today, Priscilla, unless you have something you want to add.

Priscilla:
No, I think you’ve said it so well.

Ivan:
So if you’ve listened to this podcast, I’d love to hear your thoughts. As I’ve said many times, I read every single message that goes up on BNI Podcast, and I’d like to hear about your lens or your filter that you see the world through. Because I think that lens makes all the world of difference. And it’s true in the physical world with astronomy and my telescope, and it’s true in the world of dealing with people and connecting with people. That lens you choose makes all the difference in how you view your surroundings. So let me know what you think about this podcast, I’d appreciate it. Back over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, well, I can tell you that I think it’s great. So that’s it for this week. We have a new sponsor for our podcasts and it is www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience, and help you boost your business. Whether you are a new or a seasoned Member, these audio programs will help you unlock the power of networking. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off all the audio programs [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds will go to the BNI Foundation.

So thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

Posted in Success

Comments

    Solutions-focused comments are welcome. Dr. Misner reads every comment personally.

    All comments are moderated before posting. Profanity, personal attacks, and sales pitches will be deleted.

  1. Wanda Woodard says

    June 16, 2021 at 7:53 am

    Almost six years ago, my family experienced a completely shocking, unexpected and terrible tragedy. And while the following months, after the event, were challenging and painful to say the least, I managed to move through it. My faith and my “lens” is what enabled me to do this.

    I’m human, but I have always believed that attitude determines gratitude, not the other way around. I am extremely grateful each day I wake up, I thank God for my strong health at 64, my home, my dog, my career, friends and family.

    But like you and your book, “The People In Your Room”, I’ve had to do a good deal of cleansing, getting rid of dead weight.

    BNI and my chapter in particular and our SETN BNI Executive Director Elaine Merritt, help to fill my personal and professional life with light and positive energy.

    You must be a wonderful human being, Sir, and I am so glad you had your wife with you for so many years. Thank you for creating BNI.

    Reply

  2. Colin McRae says

    June 16, 2021 at 9:35 am

    Great episode. I would add that for anybody going through a hard time, to where they can’t see anything in front of them but difficulty and a feeling of “when will this ever end” to keep in mind: it always does.

    Life is designed such that we never know what’s around the corner minute by minute, day by day. The constant effort of trying to look around that corner, which we can never actually do, is precisely what causes anxiety and all the thoughts that lead people towards hopelessness.

    Once one accepts that they can’t know what’s around that corner each day, and they start to see that living in the moment more and more is the real key to feeling safe and happy, things begin to fall into place. It’s not instantaneous, this mindset takes practice (something referred to as Mindfulness practice), but it’s the key to getting through difficult times and stopping that cycle of one’s own brain constantly looking with fear towards the future.

    And this ties back to that saying of taking one step at a time, living one day at a time. Or the analogy of when climbing a mountain, don’t look to the top, just look at the few steps in front of you. Same idea.

    We live and die, succeed and fail, entirely in our own minds, which is shaped primarily in how we CHOOSE to perceive everything going on. When you mostly focus your perception just on the present, you quite literally interrupt the process of the mind wandering off and engaging with its fears and anxiety so those things never materialize into your perceived reality.

    Of course, it’s not that easy to do, but it is that simple. Thus the need for practice. It’s a skill, like any other, requiring patience and practice, and as you improve you begin to see the benefits.

    As much as all this sounds like the monk on the mountaintop type nonsense, it’s really just the practical reality of how tough things are handled – one Step at a time. One day at a time. All things change, all hurts will heal.

    I’m spouting all this out just based on some recent experiences of my own, which I’m still working on. Hopefully someone else sees this and can it’ll inch them forward a bit on their own path to overcoming tough stuff.

    Reply

    • Richard Hall says

      June 16, 2021 at 6:06 pm

      Very poignant and well written
      Thank you

      Reply

    • Luiz Mello says

      June 22, 2021 at 12:51 pm

      Mindfulness. The Power of Now. Teachings like these are the key to life. The past and the future are both illusions, and focussing on either tends to cause suffering. Focus on the present moment is the answer.

      Reply

      • Colin McRae says

        June 23, 2021 at 9:57 am

        Sounds so simple, but takes practice because it isn’t easy, at least for me. I’m only just beginning to learn this stuff, out of necessity, but it has so far shown me that it really is the key, as you said.
        Most anxiety melts away when you just focus on the now. Seems obvious when you finally figure it out but until that point, life’s constant pulls and demands can be hard to manage. Good stuff Luiz. And btw, Habs all the way! ;)

      • Luiz Mello says

        June 23, 2021 at 2:28 pm

        You said it yourself, Colin. It is simple, but it is not easy at all. Indeed, in order master such mindset, or at least to acquire some ability to experience it, one must go against some deep-rooted patterns of thought and behavior.

        Good news is, being conscious about it is already the first step.

        And indeed good luck to your Habs!

  3. Paul Richardson says

    June 16, 2021 at 3:18 pm

    I have to admit, as a photographer, when I saw the title, I thought this podcase as going to go a different way (a successful business needs a wide-angle view, a telephoto, a macro, etc.). But this was great. Far better. Thank you for the valuable, hard one perspective, Ivan.

    There is the famous Epictetus quote I try to live by: It’s not what happens to you, but how you react to it that matters. And so I really liked that idea of “what you put in your head shows up in your world.” Kind of like who you let into your room shows up in your world…

    Keep getting in my head!

    Reply

  4. Fran Ferlazzo says

    June 16, 2021 at 5:08 pm

    Thank you for this perspective Dr. Misner. During the “Great Pause” I felt as if I was stuck in quicksand and slowly going under. It took a little longer for me to pivot my business. But like trying to steer the Titanic to miss the iceberg, it was a slow process. However, on the other side there is light. My business is more stable because of the pause, after doing the same thing for 12 years, and now I have new tools to use to be effective for those that I serve.

    Thank you to BNI for keeping my business from going totally under. It is the relationships, the caring for others and the givers gain attitude in my chapter and region that have helped a lot of us see things differently. It is my hope that we continue this trend of caring for others as it is a beautiful lens to be looking through.

    Reply

  5. Rychard Lardner says

    June 16, 2021 at 6:55 pm

    Thank you for sharing your perspective on life. I love to hear positive stories. I have a similar attitude. I have learned that I can choose to feel sorry for myself or I can choose to be happy. I now always choose the latter. I have had to have a couple of operations over the past year which required me to stay in the hospital for several days. People were feeling sorry for me and saying what a “bummer”. I knew those operations were necessary to make me better and improve my life. I was happy to be there. I also learned that the issues I had that put me in the hospital were nothing compared to the health issues that some of the other patients that I met in recovery were dealing with. I was the lucky one. The people at our office often ask me how I am feeling. My reply is I feel great and then they look at me funny when things are not going well at work. I then smile and explain that because I woke up this morning it is going to be a very good day no matter what life brings.

    Reply

  6. Robyn Nelson says

    June 16, 2021 at 9:31 pm

    Dr Misner
    I LOVE this outlook on life. I was widowed when I was 21. After the initial shock, disbelief, total devastation period, I pulled myself up, dusted myself off and decided God had a purpose for me to still be here and while I don’t know why, I have taken it upon myself to see the good in every opportunity. To live a FULL life and to smile at everyone I meet, or greet on the phone.
    I too stepped away from the news and just take micro doses of it. Early in the great pause I found the news was getting me down, and leaving me with a dark cloud over me. So I made the change so that the cloud would lift and I could again see the beauty that God surrounds us with everywhere.
    You are in my prayers as you continue to navigate life without your beautiful bride.

    Robyn

    Reply

  7. Rosemary A Bova says

    June 17, 2021 at 5:02 pm

    Chosen to comment on this podcast next week as part of my Chapter Talk its a perfect one for me. I am amazed at preponderance of negative language used in the world. It’s a life long journey to choose positivity with authenticity. I too refer to the Pandemic as the Great pause or reset. Hoping people forced out of their doing-ness would embrace Being…who are they, what they stand for.

    Like you Ivan my work is about transforming how we work. Our work systems need more balance. The business community has been unbalanced for decades. Emphasizing profitability at any cost must go. We need to bring back compassion, respect, innovation, creativity and trust in our work systems. Words carry vibrations and energy. If each of us focussed on reframing how we saw the workplace just imagine the transformation that would occur.

    Reply

  8. DEBORAH DAMESWORTH says

    June 18, 2021 at 6:35 am

    I was very moved by this podcast. During the lockdown, I refused to walk in fear. I traveled by plane to see my son and his family in FL, I had friends to my home one at a time for lunch or dinner, I took long walks every day to keep my body alert. I had more time for my devotions. I refused to let my mind be fearful. Words are important and show what we believe deep down. My husband passed in 2010, and I chose to take one step at a time, to move forward no matter who bad it hurt. I am grateful for my BNI chapter and the relationships I’ve built to keep me on track.

    Reply

  9. Wanda Woodard says

    June 18, 2021 at 8:07 am

    And, “Love”, Rosemary. We need to incorporate love into our daily work patters – love for “our” work, our fellow co-workers, our clients or patients and more.

    Reply

  10. Scott J. Malof, CPA/PFS says

    June 18, 2021 at 4:44 pm

    Ivan….I’ve been a member of the Commonwealth chapter of BNI in N. KY for 20 years. It was the chapter that Brennan Scanlon started and belonged too for about 15 of those 20 years. Man, I truly miss that guy. Anyway, I really loved this podcast. Probably my current fav. Especially the part where you talk about “words matter”. I wholeheartedly share this sentiment. The words I share with others and the words in my mental self talk matter more than almost anything else. Lastly, and hopefully without being hurtful, I would boil the narrative of this, your, podcast down to a single important acronym that I carry around at all time… PMA. Positive Mental Attitude.
    So, keep the PMA going Brother! :)

    Reply

  11. Juan Brunner says

    June 18, 2021 at 8:41 pm

    Thank you for communicating your experience and knowledge so well Dr. Misner.
    My admiration to you.

    Juan Brunner
    BNI ICON
    Guadalajara, Mexico

    Reply

  12. Bavani Sivam says

    June 19, 2021 at 10:55 pm

    Dear Ivan,

    I am Bavani Sivam from Chennai, India; From chapter Futurz – Chennai CBDB Region. I am the Educational Co-ordinator. Our Executive Director – Sunil Sethia suggested us to share BNI Podcast daily morning to educate members.

    In this process I came accross this Podcast. BNI itself is a positive thing for people and this Podcast gives a greater outlook and energy for members at this pandemic time. The Attitude of Gratitude is gives way to take up everything in a positive way in personal and professional Life.

    Thank you so much for the wonderful podcast

    Regards
    Bavani Sivam

    Reply

  13. Deyna says

    June 22, 2021 at 8:41 am

    I love this so much. I am a new BNI member, and I love the podcast and the mindset of Dr. Misner. Words matter and the law of attraction have been such a big part of my life and now in my coaching practice.
    The influence thoughts have on our emotions, and our emotions on our physical bodies are real. It is such an important part of our overall health (mental, physical, spiritual).
    Thank you, Dr. Misener!

    Reply

  14. Kitty McClellan says

    June 22, 2021 at 12:55 pm

    This podcast is filled with wisdom! I tell people all the time that we get to CHOOSE how we respond to the things that happen in our lives. So many think they don’t have any control, but they refuse to see that they DO! Be grateful, accept what is happening in your life, and keep putting one foot in front of the other. Although I lost my dream job last year during the “great pause,” another job was offered to me because of my wonderful BNI family, and now I get to help build my friend’s business and help other business owners. I am SO grateful. Start with gratitude, and it can’t help but give you a positive attitude!

    Reply

    • Colin McRae says

      June 23, 2021 at 9:53 am

      Awesome. It sure takes practice to learn to recognize when you’re giving your choice away, which is something I’m starting to learn to do, but it makes it practical and real, the option to choose that is. Great stuff about your job situation too!

      Reply

  15. guillermo diaz says

    June 22, 2021 at 2:23 pm

    Todos pasamos por pruebas y muy severas pero con la fe y personas afines a nosotros se puede salir adelante y con ejemplos de vida y lucha seguimos …asi que a tomar ventaja de las cosas positivas y con valor saldremos….. gracias BNI
    Y a todas las personas luchonas ….bendiciones

    Reply

  16. Omu Obilor says

    June 23, 2021 at 1:35 am

    I totally love this. WORDS MATTER!!!!!! I can totally relate haven done a lot of training with the book Who’s in Your ROOM. This edition is a reminder. A total life changer.

    Reply

  17. Sally M. Chetwynd says

    June 23, 2021 at 8:11 am

    Amen to this positive post! This matches exactly my personal experience.

    Recently I opened a fortune cookie to find this statement (quite a surprise, considering how inane most are): “Fear is the darkroom in which negatives are developed.”

    Nope! No interest in negativity! Haven’t got time! I’m too busy – ha ha!

    Reply

  18. Rebecca says

    June 29, 2021 at 11:28 pm

    Powerful message here! Attitude really does have a major impact in all areas of life.

    Recently I have been thinking about having fears of someday becoming “too busy”. Reframing “being busy” to “living a full life” is so tremendously helpful! Thank you for sharing this.

    On a side note- according to an email I received, today is Dr. Misner’s birthday… Happy Birthday, Ivan! Out of all the zodiac signs, I thought you were a fellow Cancer. A Cancer who overcame cancer at that :)

    Speaking of cancer, I have the book Healing Begins in the Kitchen. Such a great read, I’m a firm believer in the power to heal cancer holistically like you did. So happy for you for putting in the effort to make that happen for your self! With the right attitude you turned what could have been a tragedy into a brilliant example for others.

    Have a fulfilling birthday & thanks for all you do!

    Reply

  19. Wendy Vaught says

    June 30, 2021 at 10:22 am

    This was amazing Ivan. The world we live in now is very negative and changing constantly. I literally make it a point to be nice to people…saying Hi and being able to smile and ask someone how they are doing. We are meant to interact with one another in a positive manner. You drove it home!!! Thank you and stay well!

    Reply

    • Sally M. Chetwynd says

      June 30, 2021 at 3:14 pm

      As they say, you catch more bees with honey than with vinegar.

      Not long after I began issuing an online newsletter (last summer), I decided that a verse from the Bible was appropriate, to illustrate the newsletter’s mission:

      Ephesians 4:29: Do not let any unwholesome talk come out of your mouths, but only what is helpful for building others up according to their needs, that it may benefit those who listen.

      Negativity saps the life from everything and everyone it touches. I haven’t got time for that – got too many other really cool things to do!

      Reply

  20. Susan Nsibirwa says

    August 12, 2021 at 10:54 am

    Very inspiring. I love the idea of reframing your grief, without denying that you are going through the necessary grieving process. Words are truly very important. Thank you.

    Reply

  21. Sally M. Chetwynd says

    August 12, 2021 at 1:26 pm

    I’m reading a book by Judith Briles, a book marketing pro and inspirational speaker, called “When God Says No.” She tells how significant life events, including the loss of two children, first looked like “no’s” but later proved to be the path to “yes’s.”

    In one situation, she had been betrayed by colleagues in a big firm and driven out of her job. Several of her former clients had witnessed this and saw how wrong it was. They banded together and set up six interviews for her in a different part of the country, gave her plane tickets, and pooled money for her traveling expenses. She got one of the jobs, which set her off in a new professional direction in which she now thrives. Her “no,” fraught with despair, was turned into a “yes,” because “givers gain.” These benefactors knew how much she had helped them, so they in turn helped her when she was in need.

    Reply

  22. Andrea MacLeod says

    August 16, 2021 at 6:44 am

    Since joining BNI a few months ago, I have been working through the back catalogue of BNI podcasts and while all of them are insightful, I felt this episode really resonated with me. I always say, “Words have power”, which is why if my kids in an argument say something mean about the other, I make them then say 3 nice things about the other person right then and there. Which incidentally, cuts down on the arguments!

    But more in business, when something goes wrong, I always try and reframe it as a learning experience. As we are always learning, if something goes wrong, at least I’ve learned that’s not the way to do things next time, so I can gain what’s a positive out of an otherwise sometimes really tricky experience.

    It’s definitely a choice of how to view things in the world, and I appreciate hearing from you Dr. Misner and how you use this idea to view life. Thank you for sharing it!

    Reply

  23. Omu Obilor says

    August 9, 2022 at 10:56 pm

    Awesome. Perspectives is important.

    Reply

  24. Peter Byrnes says

    September 21, 2022 at 3:11 pm

    Dr. Misner,
    This was possibly the most meaningful podcast I have ever heard. My wife keeps telling me that my own attitude depends upon how I choose to talk about circumstances and people. And yes, evictions of unpleasant people from your life are sometimes changes that heal.

    Thanks for this.

    Reply

  25. Sushma Singh says

    March 18, 2026 at 10:08 pm

    I loved this podcast and I totally agree with this. We can not just focus on the problem that gets us nowhere . But if we focus on finding the solution with a positive attitude not only we grow but we also help people around you grow.

    I believe, it’s not that happy people do not have problems. But they are happy because they know they will win over their problems by being positive and focused on finding solutions. So the lens they choose is of courage and positivity.

    Reply

  26. Terry White says

    March 28, 2026 at 8:04 am

    Brilliant. I’m the training co-ordinator at Cheltenham, Gloucestershire, UK. I’m sure I’ve recommended this particular podcast before at the weekly meeting but it’s so good it deserves extra mentions.
    I resonate deeply with this thinking and use it constantly in my work as a life coach. I usually ease people new to me in with inviting them to change their language around “I mustn’t forget…”, instead I encourage them to say “I must remember…”, most people respond very positively to this initial approach.

    Reply

Products


  • Networking Is a Marathon, Not a Sprint     Learn how to Outlast, Outconnect, and Outperform the competition, through patience, consistency, and a giver’s mindset.

  • Infinite Legacy: The 7 Principles of Creating a Lasting Impact With Your Life

  • The 3rd Paradigm: A Radical Shift to Greater Success

  • Who's in Your Room? Revised and Updated: The Question That Will Change Your Life

  • Work Your Network With The 4Cs: Turn Every Handshake Into a Steady Stream of Referrals

  • The Connector Effect: The Proven Way to Grow Your Business Right Now

  • Infinite Giving: The 7 Principles of Givers Gain

  • The Networking Mentor: Whose Story Are You In?

  • Networking Like a Pro, 2nd Edition

  • World's Best-Known Marketing Secret

  • Healing Begins in the Kitchen: The Misner Plan

  • Avoiding the Networking Disconnect

  • 29% Solution     It's not "net-sit" or "net-eat," it's "net-WORK"

  • Business Networking and Sex (Not What You Think)     It's no surprise that communicating with the opposite sex can be tricky. Finally, help is here.

  • Masters of Success     Proven Techniques for Achieving Success in Business and Life

  • Truth or Delusion?     Busting Networking's Biggest Myths
